Welcome to Nail the Hookpoint. This is a very important module because we are getting close to getting the deal closing. Now we've been together with the audience or the buyer or the investor, whatever it may be for some good amount of time now. Let's take a look at the time sequencing just to get an understanding of how far we've come and where we are. As we got into setting the frame, we did all those things that are important to getting control and establishing status. Maybe we'll put the status line in here. When we started out, we really started from a position of no power, no strength, no authority, no status or very limited. We worked pretty hard because we didn't want to start our pitch to get up to this status point right here. That took us as good as you can get, which I may be. It takes me about 10, maybe 15 minutes. I put 10 minutes here. Then we moved into Tell the Story. Tell the Story we probably spent though 20 minutes on telling the story or giving the pitch. That moved us up to this point of status here. We know that 20 minutes, we don't want to spend longer on it because that's the limits of human attention. From there, we know we got people pretty tired. We provided some counter pressure at this point, which we made people qualify themselves to us. Tell us why we should let them be our customer, let them be in our deal. We had to reboot their brain and get them re-excited. They just were fatigued and tired at that point. If you're good at it, you spend about 10 minutes doing that. Then we moved in to discussing commercial terms. We moved in to offer the prize and put us at this status right around here. How long do we do that? Again, if you do a great job at it, about 10 minutes. Let's do a summary. At this point, we've been with the buyer or the audience for almost 50 minutes. 50 minutes is a long time to hold somebody's attention. Let's talk through this for a few moments. I think here's one way to consider it. While you are listening to these words right now and watching this screen, millions of sensory neurons in your brain are going off like blasting caps. Boom, boom, boom. All trying to knock down the wall of attention as I try and get you to focus on something new. Only a few of these blasting caps or firing neurons will break through to your conscious awareness and the rest will be ignored. It's easy enough for you to willingly let some of my messages get through. If you say, hey, I'm really going to pay attention to Oren now and to Pitch Mastery. I've been doing that for a long time. You can will yourself to let this message get through. These messages get through. In the same way that right now you're not focusing on your toes that are crammed up in the front of your shoes. But if you think about them right now and allow some attention and focus to go on them, then suddenly you're aware of them and you can feel it. You can choose what you want to let through in terms of attention. But for me trying to earn your attention, fire neurons and get it, it's much more difficult. I say this because where we are now, right here, nailing the hook point, I don't think you're going to get a lot more attention from someone after 50 minutes. You've raised their bind temperature as high as it's probably ever going to go. And this is a new subject. I want to explore bind temperature now. Let's clear the screen. Talk about bind temperature. Let's put a bind temperature line on here so you get a sense of what we're talking about. If this line is a sense of the level of excitement and interest and passion and just attention that somebody is giving you, enough to which they would say, yes, I'm interested, I'll sign up, I'll buy it, I want the deal, send me the product, I'll do the joint venture with you, let's go make this thing happen. But this line is that level at which that level of excitement is high enough. Here's what I see most people do. They walk in a room and they really start pitching. And they start to really raise bind temperature, try and generate a lot of excitement. And people are good at that. They walk in a room and get the audience excited and the buyer excited. And they get the level of excitement right up here where I'm pointing with this arrow. Okay, high enough to buy. But not enough has happened. We haven't given the pitch, right? They haven't had time to marinate in the pitch. You haven't controlled emotions. You haven't set the frame strong enough. So maybe some people are more patient. And for them, it looks like this. And they raise buying temperature some way midway through the pitch. Keep people all frothed up and excited to buy. The excitement level reaches a tizzy, right? But it's still too early to raise somebody's buying temperature. You're never going to raise somebody's buying temperature above this red line. Okay, that's when they're ready to buy. You can't keep somebody hot and excited and motivated and interested and passionate perpetually. You have to reach that point and then kind of close the deal. And if you reach that point before you're here at the hook point or ready to get the deal, then you've raised buying temperature too early. So let's keep looking at this. So maybe we'll say you're pretty good at it. And you raise buying temperature over time, right into reveal the intrigue. Okay, get people excited and passionate and motivated and you gave your great pitch and your story was there and you set the frame. It's still too early to have buying temperature hit the point that I'm ready because you haven't done enough. You haven't even offered commercial terms. You haven't even done this here. So I want to suggest this concept of controlling buying temperature in this way. Keep it under controlling and raise it using the skills we have all the way through nail the hook point. All right, so when you set the frame, it doesn't mean you're not going to go for status. Of course, we want to raise status as high as we can. That looks like this. I don't want to make this too complicated, but we want to get high status there. We want to get high status there. But buying temperature, we want to raise over time. Buying temperature, we want to raise over time until we get just past nailing the hook point. The buying temperature is here and everything is in alignment. You cannot keep someone perpetually excited to do your deal. They can't do the deal until you've offered the commercial terms. You've nailed the hook point. You've given a great pitch. Set the frame, all the stuff you need to do. So we're going to talk about that now in a little bit more detail. But this is an important concept. So important, I'm going to do a quick review. Many people will come in and they will go to get the buyer excited. I call that raising buying temperature. And they get them too excited, too fast. Haven't given the rest of the pitch yet. All of this remains to be done. So why get somebody's buying temperature to the buying point before you've given the whole pitch? They can't actually buy it that point. Other people will be a little bit more patient and they'll do it this way. Kind of wait towards the end of the pitch to get the customer really excited. But even that is too fast. Others will go like this and wait even longer until past the pitch. And I think for you, even that is going to be too fast to raise buying temperature. I think this is the mode you want to be in. It's a way to raise it perpetually, carefully, all the way to nail the hook point. Now let's get into nailing the hook point and really define that. So you can see in this shaded area, we're now in nail the hook point. Let's clear the screen. It's time to nail the hook point. Let's define that. The hook point is a singular moment in time when your audience is having an immersive experience with you. They're completely paying attention to you and into the things you're saying. They're interested in both you and the subject. They're intrigued. They're having this immersive experience with you and then they start adding value to the deal. That's the hook point. How does the buyer or the audience add value and how do you recognize that has happened? It's when they start contributing ways to make the deal stronger, the relationship better, or the transaction easier. Have you ever been in a deal and just known that the other guys wanted it? They were signaling. They wanted the deal. But what were those signals really? At those times when you just knew the buyer was ready to do the deal, it was because they started making things easier on you. They started adding value. Here's some examples of real hook points I've experienced with buyers over time. When I was helping a company sell their software to a large buyer, I heard this from the buyer. We're really excited to get the software installed. Hey, we could introduce you to our suppliers who also need this software. When you hear that from your buyer that they want to make introductions to other people they know to help you out, they're adding value and you're probably at the hook point. I heard this recently. Don't worry about getting approval from our chief financial officer. We'll make that happen so you don't have to deal with it. So at the point, if you've been through strong, set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize and now you guys are working on actually doing the deal and you hear, hey, don't worry about talking to our chief financial officer. The most painful thing you can do in the world will help make that happen so you don't have to deal with it. You are at the hook point. I heard this very recently from a company that I was working with in Geneva. They were investing in one of my deals in Southern California and they said, hey, normally we can only invest a million dollars in any deal that's outside of Europe. But we're going to break the rules here and raise our investment to three million dollars if you'll agree. So if you've offered the prize and somebody is saying, we'll break the rules to give you more money, we hope you agree with that. I think that's pretty obvious. You are at the hook point. Here's another one that you'll recognize. If your buyer says to you, hey, let's spend some social time together. We'd like to take you out to dinner to one of the nicest places in town. So when the buyer wants to take you out to dinner, you're definitely at the hook point. I heard this just recently this month, right? Hey, Orrin, if you can close in two weeks, we'll pay all the legal costs to help make this happen. The legal costs in that deal are $50,000. When somebody is willing to pay the legal costs, they're trying to make things easier on you. They're adding value and you are at the hook point. These kinds of value ads can be thought of as any action the buyer takes to sell themselves the deal, right? Doing your job for you, to negotiate against themselves, again, helping you out, making things easier for you. You'll see lots of times, guys, we'll find that's one of the ways that they can easily add value is just to negotiate against themselves. They'll concede important deal points for no benefit to themselves, or they will sell themselves to you unnecessarily. Explain why they're a good customer. Explain how they're going to help make the transaction easy. Why they're going to let you make lots of profit and how they're going to buy more and more stuff in the future. When the buyer is doing that, they're looking for ways to add value. These are a lot of the behaviors that you'll see. Where does this concept of value ad come from? Well, imagine you are pitching a deal. You're with your audience. What are you trying to do? Get attention? Yeah, of course. Tell a great story? Sure. Keep it intriguing? Yes. Time constraint. Provide fast and visual stimulus. Frame control. Right? Yes, yes, and yes, you're trying to do all that stuff. But ultimately, you're trying to add value. Trying to add value to them and their business and their lives. Remember, human interactions are all about value ad. You've heard this many times in many ways in your own career, right? What's your value ad? How do those guys add value? How is this additive? You do something for me and I'll give you money. You do something for me and I'll do something for you. It's all value exchange. If you have nothing to exchange for value, then you're dead in the water. Nobody does anything for you unless they're receiving value. Value flows back and forth in all relationships. It's just the way of things. The people you know, this value flow or value exchange is really reciprocal. I cook, you do the dishes. I change the copier toner, you take the checks to the bank. I deal with the auditors, you go to the damn customer service meeting, whatever. It's all reciprocal value exchange. Want to see how this works? Really? Stop reciprocating. Right? Just take value, but don't reciprocate it. People will stop liking you very quickly. Why? It's obvious because you're taking value without adding it in return. Value flows back and forth in all human relationships and obviously, especially in business relationships. If you haven't observed this and whatever, you just landed here from Mars five minutes ago. Business relationships are value exchange. But see, with people you don't know, people you're pitching potential JVs or customers or investors, there isn't this strong reciprocal relationship. I do the dishes you cook. There may not be any reciprocation at all. That's what I find in the beginning. In the beginning of a relationship, a new business relationship, you have to add almost all the value. After all, you're pitching them, seeking that they do something they weren't otherwise planning to do. You money or sign a contract or help you out in some way. In the beginning, you need to add all the value. So that's what strong is about, right? It's getting control of ways to add value. So you need to set the frame. They don't set the frame to your benefit. You set the frame. Then you have to tell a great story in 20 minutes. It has to be fast, visual, novel, concrete, high contrast. You have to do all that. Then after all that's done, you have to recognize they're tired. You have to reveal more intrigue. You're adding value. They're not doing anything but just sitting there and responding in ways that you've set the frame. Then you have to offer the prize. They don't do that. So you have to do all of that right up to this point. To me, this is where it's fascinating. When you have added tons of value and they're immersed in your experience, they're continuing to be intriguing. Your compelling character, your product or your service or your company is very interesting. They're immersed in your experience. They reach a tipping point inside themselves and in their mind. In their mind, this is what they are thinking. Hmm. Orrin and his team have pitched this deal. This has been very interesting. I've taken a lot of value here. I've received a lot. Orrin and his guys are great. They really care. They're considerate. They did the pitch in 20 minutes. They kept it interesting. They provided insight. They kept the pace going fast. They made their presentation novel, concrete, visual, high contrast. They created intrigue. They made us think about things we hadn't thought of before. They introduced new concepts to us. This has been colorful and this has been really refreshing. We're glad we came to this. And then what's great is they think this. They're at the tipping point. They think it's time for me to reciprocate. I got a lot for myself. It's time to add some value to this relationship. At that moment when your buyer gets the gut sense and he says to himself, it's time for me to reciprocate. I got a lot for myself and it's time to add some value to the relationship. It's at that moment that you have nailed the hook point. As I have defined the hook point, I suggested that you have nailed the hook point when two things are present. The audience or the buyer has started to add value. Really, you've been through strong. Set the frame. The real intrigue are for the prize and then they start to add value in some way. Make the relationship stronger. Make the transaction easier or concede some deal points. And they do this while they are immersed in your presentation. Then you've got to the hook point. We talked about value add. Let's talk about immersion for a minute. It's a very interesting subject. Generally, immersion is a state of mental focus so intense that awareness of the outside world is lost. It generally results in a feeling of happiness, joy, comfort, and certainly satisfaction. Another way to think about this in business, immersion is chemistry. Chemistry is when you just hit it off with another group or another person. Your anecdotes are funny. Even the unfunny ones people laugh at. The body language is great. People are open. It doesn't matter what you say. It's interpreted properly. You can say, hey guys, you're a good friend. Or you can say, hey guys, you're a fiend. It doesn't matter because they know what you mean. You're a friend. The audience knows what you mean. They're syncopated with you in phase, in step. You guys are using synchronicity. The gears are meshed. You're completing each other's sentences using extrasensory perception. You're literally mind reading each other. It's going great. This is chemistry. In fact, ad agencies have a whole phase called chemistry meetings. They literally say, hey, we're going to meet Ford or GE or Kellogg's for the chemistry meeting. This is to evaluate the chemistry between themselves and the client. The chemistry meeting drives how the rest of the relationship goes. Many times, it's the foundation for $100 million of business and a large ad agency. When I introduced the idea of immersion to a very large ad agency and that you could manufacture immersion or you could manufacture chemistry, they got very excited. Think about it for a minute. Achieving chemistry is having someone immersed in your experience. It's achieving hot cognition, which we've talked a lot about. It's taking someone all the way through strong. It's setting the frame, telling the great story, revealing the intrigue, offering the prize, and then providing an immersive experience. We're talking about getting immersion. The point of total immersion is providing the environment for the hook point to happen. It's having frame control. It's have properly managed time. Provided novelty, introducing intrigue, creating wanting. These are all things we've done up to now. The whole point of the strong exercise or program is to add value in an immersive environment and then provide them the space to add value back to you and then for you to know you've reached the hook point. Again, you're going to look for that point when they want to add value. They are going to try and make the relationship stronger. For example, hey, we can fly out to meet you. You know that you're at the hook point. That's a big effort for a buyer or audience or someone to fly out to meet you. They want to make the transaction easier. I've heard this before. Hey, look, let's not wait for legal to sort all this out. We'll send a check today to get the transaction started. I once received a $2.1 million investment before any paperwork was drafted. That's a good sign that you've reached the hook point when you actually get the money. Another way they're going to add value, which we said before, the terms of the deal get better. I heard this just last month. Let's not argue about price anymore. Let's just make it $30 million even and we'll move on and get this done. That's obviously the hook point. Let's not argue about price. Let's just make it a round number that's fair to everyone. That is the hook point. My last thought on this is where do you find immersion or how do you make sure you're creating it? Immersion is at the crossroads of experiencing and thinking. It's at the center of feeling and analyzing. It's at the intersection of perception and cognition. Let's not get choked out on these words, but they are very important to understand. Let's define a few things so we can go deeper into this idea of immersion. How do you get immersion? The way to get into this subject is really to understand two things. Perceptual systems and cognitive systems. I don't like jargon any more than you do. Let's get through this quickly. Perceptual systems are really easy. Perception is our senses. It's what we take in from the world around us. Cognitive systems are our internal systems. It's what we think. It's the frames, cognitive frames. It's our internal thinking. Perception is the process of receiving information from our senses and categorizing it inside our minds. Think about your brain for a moment. It's dark in your skull. There's no direct link to the outside world. The brain has no ability to feel, see, or hear. It must rely on information from your organs, your eyes, ears, and skin. Perception is the organization, identification, and interpretation of information in order to build an understanding of what is going on outside the body. All perception involves signals from the nervous system, which in turn result from physical stimulation of the sense organs. For example, vision involves light striking the retinas of the eye. Smell is caused by odor molecules and hearing involves pressure waves. Perception is not the passive receipt of these signals. Perception is the interpretation of signals and the making of meaning. We perceive the world around us in order to drive actions favorable to survival. You may believe the purpose of perception is knowledge, but evolutionary psychologists hold that its primary purpose is to guide action. For example, depth perception seems to have evolved not to help us know the distances to other objects, but rather to help us move around in space. We use eyesight for collision avoidance, suggesting that vision is basically for directing action, not providing knowledge. Perception is used to help us take action. More than half the brain is devoted to processing sensory information, and the brain itself consumes roughly a quarter of your metabolic resources. Therefore, the senses must provide exceptional benefits to fitness and survival. Perception accurately mirrors the world. Humans get useful, accurate information through their senses so they can take action. In business, activating the perceptual system or feeding the senses new stimuli gives people raw data. But there's a limited interpretation of this data during perception. There's not much knowledge happening, if any, at all. During perception, it's just move, assess environment, survive, move. Cognition literally means to know. Cognitive processes are decoupled from your physical environment. By contrast, you cannot decouple perception from your physical environment. But you don't need new information to think. Knowledge can be thought of as problem solving, and reaching conclusions from doing the act of thinking. Before, we said the brain is stuck in a black box. There's no light, sound, or smell in there. That's just fine for thinking, which is the job of manipulating previously stored images, sounds, symbols, and emotions. Very simplified, perception creates a data set for the brain to manipulate. Cognition creates knowledge, ideas, and solutions from this data. The term cognition refers to all processes by which the sensory input is transformed, reduced, elaborated, stored, recovered, and used. Cognition is thinking, and thinking can happen even in the absence of new sensory stimulation. So when perceptual systems and cognitive systems are undertaxed, people become apathetic and bored. When you're not giving people a lot of sensory input, and you're not making them think very hard, that's called undertaxing their perceptual and cognitive systems. And your audience becomes apathetic and bored. Now similarly, if people are overtaxed, if you've given too much perceptual or sensory input, and you've made people think too hard, they become overtaxed. And that leads to stress and frustration. So immersion happens between these two states when perceptual and cognitive systems are challenged at near capacity without being exceeded. Under these conditions, the audience loses a sense of the real world or the outside world, and they typically experience intense feelings of, as I said before, joy and satisfaction. Immersion is characterized by the following four things. You've given your audience challenges that can be overcome. You've given them a feeling of control over the situation they're in and how they're learning interesting things. They have a loss of concern over matters of the self, so they're not thinking about being thirsty or hungry. And they have a modified sense of time. Hours pass, and it feels like minutes. So let's talk about immersion a little bit more in terms of these concepts, perception, perceptual inputs, and cognitive inputs. Green park rides, Six Flags, Great Adventure, whatever, they can be totally immersive, but they're overly loaded on the perceptual side. There's no cognitive or thinking stimulation. It doesn't fall between the two states. Now complex games like chess can be immersive, but they're completely cognitively loaded. It's all about thinking. There's no movement. There's no sensory perception. It's all lost in your mind thinking about the game you're in. Both of those are immersive environments, but they're not conducive to creating wanting or reaching the hook point. It's very easy to build environments that achieve immersion through perceptual stimulation. So those are theme park rides or going to a motorcycle or monster truck race or going to a football game, but those are brief experiences and they're not good for pitching. The optimal immersive experience involves rich sensory experiences and rich cognitive engagement. Again, the audience should experience challenges that can be overcome, environments that minimize distractions are good for reaching the hook point, having an immersive experience, promote a feeling of control. See that's why I hate selling. It takes the control away from the buyer. They feel constantly like you're manipulating them. When you're creating an immersive environment, you want to emphasize stimulus that are warm or hot versus cold and analytical. And really you want to help people disconnect from reality or the outside world for the period of time that you have them. Think about building the future with you, your product, your joint venture, your investment. Those are immersive experiences. So along the way, having done all these things and in the modules S, the module T and R revealed the intrigue O offer the prize, there were many things to do. But I will say it this way, you will need to have hit certain waypoints during the lead up to the hook point. So here's a new term. Waypoints are a set of coordinates that identify a point in physical space. So traditionally a waypoint is for identifying a point in physical space. Waypoints have traditionally been associated with distinctive features in the real world, rock formations, mountains, streams, that's tree clumps. But today waypoints, I mean obviously are manufactured specifically for navigation. Waypoints are radio beacons, buoys in the ocean, satellites, GPS markers. In the modern world, the idea of waypoints has become increasingly abstract. So we're going to talk about here. Often they have no obvious relationship or any distinctive features in the real world. These abstract waypoints are used to help define invisible routing paths for navigation. For example, artificial airways or you know, highways in the sky are created specifically for the purposes of air navigation. And they have no real clear connection to the features of the real world. And really highways in the sky or where airplanes are allowed to go only consist of a series of abstract waypoints through which pilots have to navigate. So our airways which keep us safe when we fly are designed to facilitate air traffic control and the routing of traffic between heavily traveled locations only by waypoints. And there's no pilots have no reference really to natural terrain features. So now a definition. A waypoint is a fixed point along a planned course used to make a journey or simply a point of reference useful for navigation. Now let's bring it into the pitch. This concept of waypoints nine out of 10 times. We're only going to get to the hook point when we've gone through a series of cognitive waypoints. So here we've put up the strong model with the status line running through it and I've listed the 14 waypoints. Let's discuss them and kind of look at where they go chronologically through the pitch. So the big idea of course you're going to be somewhere is going to be somewhere in this area. It's a waypoint you have to hit. Prizing you're going to be doing that all the way along setting the frame. Certainly you're going to see the strongest amount of prizing or counter pressure when you've finished giving your pitch. A social engagement. You're going to see a lot of social engagement along here while you're setting the frame. Certainly when you're revealing the intrigue. I mean it's impossible to get to nailing the hook point without creating fun and laughter and social engagement. Come on guys this stuff has got to be fun for the audience. Why would they be there otherwise? That's one of our tenants right? We're always having fun. So if we want to have fun we've got to give it to the audience as well. Frame control. Somewhere in here you're going to perpetrate a denial or a defiance and certainly down this way when you get to offer the prize. You're going to be getting some static, some questions, some feedback. You're going to need to get frame control back again. You're going to do that with a denial and a defiance. So we've talked about tension. Tensions are a waypoint you're going to have to go through. High point of tension here when you apply counter pressure. High point of tension here and offer the prize. And maybe right when you walk in the room I like to do it. Hey guys good to meet you. Oh I see there's a lot of billionaires in this room and 100 millionaires and I'm very happy to have all of you here. Now let me decide who I'm going to kick off the island first. Who should I ask to leave? Very tense. Tension is a waypoint you have to go through. You're going to build a character we know specifically where you're going to build a compelling character. That's right there at the beginning of your pitch. If you don't do that you haven't passed that waypoint. It's going to be hard to nail the hook point later on. Again when you build a compelling character it's going to be here. It's a waypoint you have to go through. Novelty and intrigue. Very powerful. It's obvious all the way through here. That's what you're going to be passing through that waypoint. Giving a clear value proposition. If you have not done that it's going to be very hard to nail the hook point. You know give a clear value proposition somewhere in here during your pitch. A generic solution. It's another waypoint you've got to go through. That's obviously going to be somewhere through your pitch. It's going to be in this area. A crock friendly pitch. Fast, novel, visual, high contrast and concrete. That's a waypoint. It's going to be right in here. Apply counter pressure. We know exactly where that is. We've discussed it many times. Right there. We need information along the way of giving a pitch or making a sale. We want to avoid asking lots of questions. We make statements that the audience or the buyer or the investor will engage with. We make statements. So that's probably in here while we're having a bunch of conversation. Definitely in here. That is a waypoint that you will go through. Not asking questions to gain information but making interesting statements and having the audience engage with you. If that has not happened you have not passed through that waypoint. You can't really get to nail the hook point. You need to have suggested you'll walk away if the deal is not right for you. That probably happened here and here and here and here and here and here and here and here and here. If you haven't gone through that waypoint six or seven times I'll be surprised. Of course you're going to constrain time. As a waypoint you will go through. You may have done it as I do right here. Hey guys I got about 20 minutes to give you the pitch. We'll have some time for Q&A. Maybe we'll have an hour to spend together. I have to bounce. I have another meeting. Definitely we'll do time, retime, constrain there. Probably happens here and here and here. So that's an easy waypoint to go through. Again I'll define it. A waypoint is a fixed point along the planned course used to make a journey or simply a point of reference useful for navigation. So if you want to navigate yourself over here to the hookpoint you will pass through these 14 waypoints and then you'll be in immersive experience here in the last 10 or 15 minutes of your meeting and you'll be nailing the hookpoint. It's where the chemistry is perfect. So that's it. Thanks for watching. I'll see you guys in the next video. Bye. Bye. So let's continue this conversation by waypoints and we pause for a minute and say this. I did some training recently with these Navy SEALs out of Las Vegas. It was kind of this day long combat training. All very interesting. But I learned a couple things from the exercises from the Navy SEALs. For business people this stuff is mainly a curiosity. Number one, Navy SEALs, their number one objective is they keep their priorities in order. They know what comes first, second, third. It's kind of fortune cookie wisdom but they seem to apply that. And then the second thing is no one to act without hesitation. Kind of melodramatic. In a boardroom I've never felt like I had to act in one second without hesitation. I mean certainly when people say things about my deal that tries to weaken it, they say we will close distance and shut it down. But if we don't react in the first two seconds, I mean it's not life or death. But there's a third thing I found very interesting from this fighting training with these SEALs and I think it's interesting and applicable. So these guys, these Navy SEALs, they train to recognize the results of an action they take. For example, they kick someone in the nuts. They look for something very specific to indicate that they've done it properly. And what will happen is when you kick someone in the nuts properly their arms will fling out and their butt will kind of sink back. That's the social biology of being kicked there because it's our reproductive system. So our arms fling out to protect that a second blow from happening. We move our butt backwards to create some distance to wherever that blow came. So our arms fling out, our butt moves backwards. So they look for that reaction. If they don't see it, they know they haven't achieved the result. Another example, if they go to break someone's ankle, not something I ever imagined doing or ever will need to do, the training was fun. But if they go to break somebody's ankle, they'll look for a very specific reaction. Both hands will grab the ankle and the victim will double over in a certain way. If they don't see that reaction, then they know they didn't get the result and they have to go back in and try it again. This applies to us too. So as you progress through strong, set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize and nail the hook point and you get to these waypoints. You're going to be looking for the results of your actions. So let's talk through some of those. When you have delivered the big idea properly, in my experience, over hundreds of pitches, here's what you're going to see as a result. Sudden attention, people are going to glance, put down their phones, stop texting. They're going to lean forward and they're going to nod appreciatively. So when you really deliver the big idea, that's the result that you should see. When you prize properly, you're not going to see a lot of outward result from the audience, but there is another result you should detect. You will feel a strong inner sense of control. So you know you're prizing properly, not when you see something from the audience, but within you, kind of the beast rises and you say, yes, I've got some control over myself. Whatever happens, I'm not going to react to the audience, but I know that I've had the courage and the strength to prize myself, to take the, commoditize them and say their money is a commodity. What I have, my product, my service, myself, is what's the prize in this room and you'll feel that inner sense of control rise within you and you know that's the result you've passed to the waypoint. When you have got social engagement, you've made it fun. I mean, it's a no brainer, right? You're going to see smiling from the audience, their body language is going to open. I'm not a big body language guy. I want to spend all day evaluating body language, but it's a no brainer, right? It's social engagement. People are having fun. They're kind of arms open up. They turn towards you. They're smiling, nodding appreciatively. When you get frame control, right? You perpetrate a denial or a defiance. The audience or the buyers reacting to you. It's the very definition of frame control that we've been through many times. You have frame control when someone is reacting to you in any way and they have frame control when you are reacting to them. So, when you pass to the waypoint of having gotten frame control, obviously, when you laugh, they laugh. When you stand up, maybe they stand up and follow you. When you move to a different part of the room to draw something on the board, their gaze follows you or they turn. When people are reacting to you, you have frame control and you know you've gone through that waypoint. Tension. When you have created tension, what you will see is you will get a lot of attention. The focus will be back on you. You've recaptured attention. That's the result you'll get having gone through the tension waypoint. When you build a character, shown that you've done something important, it was difficult, required difficult decisions in high stakes, circumstances. I'm not sure there's a result to measure from the audience. They should look, continue to look interested. That one's hard, but it's still a waypoint you have to go through. When you correctly go through the waypoint of mixing novelty and intrigue, again, you have captured attention. People will, their body will turn towards you. Their gaze will look more towards your face. Not a big body language, guys, as we've said, but you feel attention when it's on you. When you deliver a clear value proposition, you go through the waypoint in your pitch. When you're telling a story, giving the pitch, of giving a clear value proposition. When you get through that, it will feel like you've just weaved through three basketball players on a difficult course and dunked the ball. You will have a great self-appreciation. May not see a great reaction from the audience, but again, it will well up inside you, a sense of self-appreciation. You feel like you've done something great. You will feel it. When you give a clear value proposition in front of an audience, there will be a reaction from within you. When you give a crock-friendly pitch, fast, novel, visual, high contrast, and concrete, and you pass through that waypoint, there will be lots and lots of attentional cues from the audience. They will turn towards you. They will open up their body language. They will lean forward. They will submit interesting comments that support the conversation, and they may ask some questions that are not generic, that really shows their listening and are interested. When you suggest that you will walk away from the deal if it's not good for you, that's a waypoint you have to go through. You might go through that several times. When you suggest that you'll walk away if it doesn't benefit you, if they're not a good customer, if they're not a good buyer, if they're not going to be a good investor, you will see two things happening. One, it will introduce tension to the room, get the focus on you, and at the same time you'll feel an internal self-satisfaction. You've prized yourself. You have enough backbone to say, if this deal isn't good for me, I'm going to walk. Those are some of the results you'll look for. The workbook will go deeper into that, but you'll know exactly what to look for when you go through these waypoints. That way you know you've achieved the result. If you don't see the result, you better find a way to go through it again. If you want to nail the hookpoint, have awesome chemistry, provide an immersive experience just before you get the deal, the thing locks down. These are some of the waypoints you have to go through.
